questions.question:
Which of the following statements are TRUE?
I. Shortest remaining time first scheduling may cause starvation
II. Preemptive scheduling may cause starvation
III. Round robin is better than FCFS in terms of response time

A. I only
B. I and III only
C. II and III only
D. I, II and III
questions.to_find: Which of the given statements about CPU scheduling are true

understand.plain: We are asked to evaluate three statements about CPU scheduling algorithms. SRTF is preemptive SJF, Round Robin gives each process a time quantum, and FCFS runs processes in arrival order. We need to check whether each statement is correct.

keywords.explain: A process is starved when it waits indefinitely because higher-priority or shorter processes keep arriving and getting scheduled ahead of it.
keywords.example: In SRTF, a long process may never run if short processes keep arriving.

keywords.explain: A scheduling algorithm that gives each ready process a fixed time slice (quantum) in circular order, ensuring every process gets CPU time regularly.
keywords.example: With quantum 2ms: P1 runs 2ms, then P2 runs 2ms, then P1 again, etc.

keywords.explain: The time from when a process arrives until it first gets CPU service (first response).
keywords.example: If P1 arrives at t=0 and first runs at t=4, its response time is 4.

understand.visual_alt: Three scheduling statements being evaluated: SRTF starvation, preemptive starvation, RR vs FCFS response time

given.aim: Determine which of the three scheduling statements are true

terms.term: Statement I: SRTF may cause starvation
terms.meaning: Shortest Remaining Time First is preemptive SJF; long processes can be perpetually preempted by shorter ones
terms.example: A 100ms process never runs if 5ms processes keep arriving
terms.connects: TRUE — SRTF favors short processes, long ones can starve

terms.term: Statement II: Preemptive scheduling may cause starvation
terms.meaning: Any preemptive scheme (priority-based, SRTF) can starve lower-priority/longer processes
terms.example: In preemptive priority scheduling, low-priority processes may never run
terms.connects: TRUE — SRTF itself is preemptive and causes starvation, so preemptive scheduling CAN cause starvation

terms.term: Statement III: RR is better than FCFS in response time
terms.meaning: FCFS can make a short process wait behind a long one (convoy effect); RR limits max wait to (n-1)*quantum
terms.example: With 3 processes, FCFS might make P3 wait 20ms; RR makes P3 wait at most 2*quantum
terms.connects: TRUE — RR has bounded response time, FCFS does not

given.plan: Evaluate each statement independently using scheduling theory. Statement I is true (SRTF starves long jobs). Statement II is true (SRTF is preemptive and causes starvation). Statement III is true (RR gives every process a turn quickly, so response time is bounded and better than FCFS on average).

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I: SRTF may cause starvation
steps.apply: SRTF always schedules the process with the shortest remaining burst. If a stream of short processes continuously arrives, a long process may never get CPU time. This is the classic starvation scenario. Statement I is TRUE.
steps.note: Starvation in SRTF is addressed by aging (increasing priority over time).

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II: Preemptive scheduling may cause starvation
steps.apply: Since SRTF is itself a preemptive scheduling algorithm and it causes starvation (as shown in Step 1), preemptive scheduling in general may cause starvation. Statement II is TRUE.
steps.note: Not all preemptive algorithms guarantee starvation — RR is preemptive but starvation-free. The statement says 'may cause', which is true.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement III: RR is better than FCFS in response time
steps.apply: FCFS has a convoy effect: one long process blocks all shorter ones behind it, giving large response times. RR with quantum q ensures every process gets its first slice within (n-1)*q time. On average, RR significantly reduces response time compared to FCFS. Statement III is TRUE.
steps.note: RR may have worse turnaround time than FCFS for short processes due to context-switching overhead, but response time is better.

steps.title: Combine results
steps.apply: All three statements (I, II, III) are TRUE. The answer is D.
steps.note: This is a standard GATE question testing conceptual knowledge of scheduling properties.

solution.result: D. I, II and III — all three statements are true

questions.formula_note: Properties of SRTF (starvation of long processes), preemptive scheduling (can starve), and Round Robin (bounded response time better than FCFS convoy effect)

questions.question:
Consider the following table of arrival time and burst time for three processes P0, P1 and P2.

Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time
P0      |     0 ms     |    9 ms
P1      |     1 ms     |    4 ms
P2      |     2 ms     |    9 ms

The pre-emptive shortest job first scheduling algorithm is used. Scheduling is carried out only at arrival or completion of processes. What is the average waiting time for the three processes?

A. 5.0 ms
B. 4.33 ms
C. 6.33 ms
D. 7.33 ms
questions.to_find: Average waiting time under preemptive SJF (SRTF) scheduling

understand.plain: Three processes arrive at different times with different burst times. We use Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF), which is preemptive SJF. At each arrival or completion event, the process with the shortest remaining burst time runs. We compute how long each process waits.

keywords.explain: Preemptive version of SJF. At every scheduling event, the process with the minimum remaining burst time is selected. If a new process arrives with a shorter burst than what is currently running, it preempts.
keywords.example: P0 has 8ms remaining when P1 arrives with 4ms, so P1 preempts P0.

keywords.explain: Total time a process spends in the ready queue waiting for CPU, not counting its own execution time or the time before it arrived.
keywords.example: If P0 arrives at 0, finishes at 13, and has burst 9, its waiting time = 13 - 9 - 0 = 4ms.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 620 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='30' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='25' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P0</text><rect x='40' y='10' width='30' height='30' class='ok' opacity='0.7'/><text x='55' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>P1</text><rect x='70' y='10' width='120' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P0</text><rect x='190' y='10' width='270' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='325' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P2</text><text x='10' y='65' class='sub ink'>0</text><text x='38' y='65' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='68' y='65' class='sub ink'>5</text><text x='185' y='65' class='sub ink'>13</text><text x='455' y='65' class='sub ink'>22</text><text x='10' y='90' class='sub mut'>Gantt Chart: P0(0-1) | P1(1-5) | P0(5-13) | P2(13-22)</text><text x='10' y='115' class='sub ink'>P0 wait = 13-9-0 = 4ms | P1 wait = 5-4-1 = 0ms | P2 wait = 22-9-2 = 11ms</text><text x='10' y='140' class='lbl ink'>Average = (4+0+11)/3 = 5.0 ms</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Gantt chart: P0 runs 0-1, P1 preempts and runs 1-5, P0 resumes 5-13, P2 runs 13-22. Waiting times: P0=4, P1=0, P2=11, average=5.

given.aim: Find average waiting time under SRTF for P0, P1, P2

terms.meaning: Process 0 arrives first with the longest burst
terms.example: Starts at t=0, gets preempted at t=1 when P1 arrives
terms.connects: Runs 0-1, then waits until t=5 when P1 finishes, then runs 5-13

terms.meaning: Process 1 arrives at t=1 with burst 4 < P0's remaining 8
terms.example: Preempts P0 at t=1 and runs uninterrupted until t=5
terms.connects: Zero wait time (runs immediately on arrival)

terms.meaning: Process 2 arrives at t=2 with burst 9 > P1's remaining 3
terms.example: Cannot preempt P1; must wait in queue
terms.connects: Waits from t=2 until t=13 when P0 finishes, then runs 13-22

given.plan: Simulate SRTF event by event: at each arrival or completion, compare remaining burst times and schedule the shortest. Track when each process actually executes to compute waiting time = finish_time - burst - arrival_time.

steps.title: Simulate SRTF Gantt chart
steps.apply:
t=0: Only P0 ready, starts running (remaining=9).
t=1: P1 arrives (burst=4). P0 remaining=8 > P1 burst=4, so P1 preempts P0. P1 runs.
t=2: P2 arrives (burst=9). P1 remaining=3 < P2 burst=9, so P1 continues.
t=5: P1 completes. Ready queue: P0(remaining=8), P2(remaining=9). P0 has shorter remaining, runs from t=5.
t=13: P0 completes. Only P2 in queue. P2 runs from t=13 to t=22.
Gantt: [P0: 0-1] [P1: 1-5] [P0: 5-13] [P2: 13-22]
steps.note: Scheduling events occur only at arrivals (t=1, t=2) and completions (t=5, t=13, t=22).

steps.title: Calculate waiting times
steps.formula_raw: Waiting time = Completion time - Burst time - Arrival time
steps.apply:
P0: completion=13, burst=9, arrival=0 => wait = 13-9-0 = 4 ms
P1: completion=5, burst=4, arrival=1 => wait = 5-4-1 = 0 ms
P2: completion=22, burst=9, arrival=2 => wait = 22-9-2 = 11 ms
steps.note: P1 has zero wait because it immediately preempts on arrival.

solution.result: A. 5.0 ms — the average waiting time is 5.0 ms

questions.formula_note: SRTF (preemptive SJF): at each event, schedule the process with minimum remaining burst. Waiting time = Completion - Burst - Arrival.

questions.question:
Consider the 3 processes, P1, P2 and P3 shown in the table.

Process | Arrival Time | Time Units Required
P1      |      0       |         3
P2      |      1       |         6
P3      |      4       |         4

The completion order of the 3 processes under the policies FCFS and RR2 (round robin scheduling with CPU quantum of 2 time units) are:

A. FCFS: P1, P2, P3    RR2: P1, P3, P2
B. FCFS: P1, P3, P2    RR2: P1, P3, P2
C. FCFS: P1, P2, P3    RR2: P1, P2, P3
D. FCFS: P1, P3, P2    RR2: P1, P2, P3
questions.answer: A. FCFS: P1, P2, P3    RR2: P1, P3, P2
questions.to_find: Completion order of processes under FCFS and Round Robin (quantum=2)

understand.plain: Three processes with different arrival times and burst times are scheduled under two policies: FCFS (runs each process to completion in arrival order) and RR2 (gives each process 2 time units at a time in round-robin). We trace the Gantt chart for each policy and find the order of completion.

keywords.explain: Non-preemptive scheduling that runs processes in the order they arrive, completing each one before starting the next.
keywords.example: P1 arrives first, runs fully, then P2, then P3.

keywords.explain: Each process runs for at most q time units, then is preempted and placed at the back of the ready queue. Ensures fairness.
keywords.example: With q=2: P1 runs 2 units, P2 runs 2 units, P1 runs remaining 1 unit, etc.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 620 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='25' class='lbl ink'>FCFS:</text><rect x='70' y='10' width='45' height='25' class='hi'/><text x='92' y='27' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P1</text><rect x='115' y='10' width='90' height='25' class='ok' opacity='0.7'/><text x='160' y='27' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:#fff'>P2</text><rect x='205' y='10' width='60' height='25' class='bg'/><text x='235' y='27' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>P3</text><text x='70' y='50' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='112' y='50' class='sub mut'>3</text><text x='202' y='50' class='sub mut'>9</text><text x='262' y='50' class='sub mut'>13</text><text x='10' y='80' class='sub ink'>FCFS order: P1(finishes t=3), P2(finishes t=9), P3(finishes t=13) => P1,P2,P3</text><text x='10' y='110' class='lbl ink'>RR2:</text><text x='10' y='135' class='sub ink'>P1(0-2),P2(2-4),P1(4-5),P3(5-7),P2(7-9),P3(9-11),P2(11-13)</text><text x='10' y='160' class='sub ink'>P1 finishes at 5, P3 finishes at 11, P2 finishes at 13</text><text x='10' y='185' class='lbl ink'>RR2 order: P1, P3, P2</text></svg>

given.aim: Determine process completion order under FCFS and RR2

terms.example: Under FCFS runs 0-3; under RR2 runs 0-2 then 4-5
terms.connects: Completes first in both FCFS (t=3) and RR2 (t=5)

terms.meaning: Arrives second with the largest burst
terms.example: Under FCFS waits for P1 then runs 3-9
terms.connects: Completes second in FCFS (t=9), but last in RR2 (t=13)

terms.example: Under FCFS must wait for P1 and P2 to complete
terms.connects: Completes last in FCFS (t=13), second in RR2 (t=11)

given.plan: Simulate both schedulers step by step. For FCFS, process in arrival order non-preemptively. For RR2, maintain a ready queue, give each process at most 2 units, add new arrivals to the back of the queue.

steps.title: Simulate FCFS
steps.apply:
t=0: P1 arrives, runs.
t=1: P2 arrives, waits (P1 running, FCFS is non-preemptive).
t=3: P1 completes. P2 runs (P3 not yet arrived).
t=4: P3 arrives, waits (P2 running).
t=9: P2 completes. P3 runs.
t=13: P3 completes.
Completion order under FCFS: P1 (t=3), P2 (t=9), P3 (t=13) => P1, P2, P3
steps.note: FCFS is non-preemptive, so later-arriving short processes cannot jump ahead.

steps.title: Simulate RR2 (quantum=2)
steps.apply:
t=0: Queue=[P1]. P1 runs for min(2,3)=2 units. P1 remaining=1.
t=2: P2 has arrived (at t=1). Queue=[P2, P1(rem=1)]. P2 runs 2-4. P2 remaining=4.
t=4: P3 arrives. Queue=[P1(rem=1), P3(rem=4), P2(rem=4)]. P1 runs 4-5. P1 remaining=0. P1 DONE at t=5.
t=5: Queue=[P3(rem=4), P2(rem=4)]. P3 runs 5-7. P3 remaining=2.
t=7: Queue=[P2(rem=4), P3(rem=2)]. P2 runs 7-9. P2 remaining=2.
t=9: Queue=[P3(rem=2), P2(rem=2)]. P3 runs 9-11. P3 remaining=0. P3 DONE at t=11.
t=11: Queue=[P2(rem=2)]. P2 runs 11-13. P2 DONE at t=13.
Completion order under RR2: P1 (t=5), P3 (t=11), P2 (t=13) => P1, P3, P2
steps.note: When a process uses its full quantum and a new process has arrived in the same time step, the new arrival goes behind the just-preempted process in standard RR. At t=4, P3 arrives exactly when P1 gets its turn — P3 joins the queue after P1.

steps.title: Match results to options
steps.apply:
FCFS completion order: P1, P2, P3
RR2 completion order: P1, P3, P2
This matches option A.
steps.note: The key difference: P2's large burst causes it to finish last under RR2 but second under FCFS.

solution.result: A. FCFS: P1, P2, P3   RR2: P1, P3, P2

questions.formula_note: FCFS non-preemptive simulation and Round Robin with quantum 2; completion order depends on how time slices interleave with burst sizes and arrival times.

questions.question:
A scheduling algorithm assigns priority proportional to the waiting time of a process. Every process starts with priority zero (the lowest priority). The scheduler re-evaluates the process priorities every T time units and decides the next process to schedule. Which one of the following is TRUE if the processes have no I/O operations and all arrive at time zero?

A. This algorithm is equivalent to the first-come first-served algorithm.
B. This algorithm is equivalent to the round-robin algorithm.
C. This algorithm is equivalent to the shortest-job-first algorithm.
D. This algorithm is equivalent to the shortest-remaining-time-first algorithm.
questions.answer: B. This algorithm is equivalent to the round-robin algorithm.
questions.to_find: Which standard scheduling algorithm is equivalent to the described priority-proportional-to-waiting-time algorithm

understand.plain: A custom scheduler assigns priority equal to a process's current waiting time, re-evaluates every T time units, and picks the highest-priority (longest-waiting) process. All processes arrive at t=0 with priority 0 and have no I/O. We need to identify which classic algorithm this behaves like.

keywords.term: Aging / Priority Proportional to Waiting Time
keywords.explain: A technique where priority increases with waiting time, preventing starvation. Here it IS the scheduling policy itself.
keywords.example: At re-evaluation, the process that has waited longest has the highest priority and gets scheduled.

keywords.explain: Each process gets equal CPU time slices (quantum T) in rotation, ensuring all processes are served fairly with the same time slice.
keywords.example: With quantum T=5ms and 3 processes: P1 runs 5ms, P2 runs 5ms, P3 runs 5ms, P1 again...

understand.visual_alt: All processes start at t=0 with priority 0. After each T units, the process that waited the longest gets scheduled — this cycles through all processes equally, matching Round Robin behavior.

given.aim: Identify which classic scheduling algorithm the described algorithm is equivalent to when all processes arrive at t=0

terms.meaning: Every process starts simultaneously with waiting time 0
terms.example: At t=0 all n processes have priority 0 (equal), so one is chosen arbitrarily
terms.connects: After the first T units, the waiting processes have all waited T units equally

terms.meaning: The scheduler picks the process with max waiting time at each T-interval boundary
terms.example: At t=T: 1 process ran (waited 0), n-1 waited T units — the n-1 waiting ones have higher priority
terms.connects: Each T-unit interval one process runs; the rest accumulate waiting time and get scheduled in rotation

terms.meaning: A process that has waited longer gets scheduled next
terms.example: Among processes that have never run, all have waited equally; the scheduler picks in round-robin order
terms.connects: Forces fair rotation with equal T-unit slices, identical to RR with quantum T

given.plan: Trace what happens with n processes all arriving at t=0. At t=0, all have priority 0 — one (say P1) is chosen. At t=T, P1 has waited 0, all others waited T. So any of P2..Pn is chosen (say P2). At t=2T, P2 waited 0, P1 waited T, P3..Pn waited 2T — a process from P3..Pn is chosen. This produces a round-robin rotation where each gets exactly T time units.

steps.title: Analyze the state at each re-evaluation
steps.apply:
At t=0: all n processes have priority 0. Suppose P1 is selected (ties broken arbitrarily).
At t=T: P1 has run T units (waiting time = 0). All other processes have waited T units (priority = T). Any of P2..Pn is selected — say P2.
At t=2T: P2 has run T units (waiting = 0). P1 has now waited T units (priority = T). P3..Pn have waited 2T (priority = 2T). One of P3..Pn is selected.
steps.note: In general, the process with the longest continuous wait always wins next. This naturally cycles through all processes giving each T units.

steps.title: Identify the equivalent algorithm
steps.apply: Each process receives exactly T time units of CPU in each cycle before any other process gets a second slice. The rotation order respects 'longest waiting first', which in the all-arrive-at-t=0 scenario simply cycles through all processes in order. This is exactly Round Robin with time quantum T.
steps.note: If processes had different arrival times or I/O, the behavior might differ from strict RR, but under the given conditions (all at t=0, no I/O) it is equivalent.

steps.title: Rule out other options
steps.apply:
A (FCFS): FCFS runs each process to completion, not in T-unit slices — ruled out.
C (SJF): SJF picks shortest burst, not longest waiting — ruled out.
D (SRTF): SRTF picks shortest remaining time — ruled out.
B (RR): Matches exactly — each process gets T-unit slices in rotation.
steps.note: This is a conceptual GATE question about understanding what priority=waiting-time translates to in practice.

solution.result: B. This algorithm is equivalent to the round-robin algorithm.

questions.formula_note: When priority is proportional to waiting time and all processes arrive simultaneously with no I/O, the algorithm degenerates to Round Robin with quantum T, since all non-running processes accumulate waiting time equally.
